mechanism_narrative: Declining payroll employment (PAYEMS) signals labor market deterioration that reduces consumer spending capacity and corporate earnings expectations, producing negative S&P 500 forward returns over 20 trading days. The mechanism operates through reduced aggregate demand and profit margin compression.
evidence_refs: fact:July 2009 labor market still recovering from 2008 crisis
failure_condition: Monthly PAYEMS declines do not precede negative 20-day SPX returns in > 55% of instances over a 3-year rolling window, or Rank IC < 0.04 in any 2-year period.

mechanism_narrative: The federal funds rate at elevated levels (FEDFUNDS > 5%) relative to inflation signals monetary tightening that raises discount rates on future corporate cash flows, depressing equity valuations and producing negative S&P 500 returns over 5 trading days as market reprices risk.
regime_hint: monetary tightening cycle with inflation persistence
failure_condition: Triggered instances produce positive 5-day SPX returns in > 50% of cases, or Rank IC < 0.02 over any 18-month rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Rising retail sales (RSAFS) indicate strengthening consumer demand and corporate revenue growth, which translates to positive earnings revisions and S&P 500 appreciation over 60 trading days as the market reprices growth expectations upward.
failure_condition: RSAFS growth does not precede positive 60-day SPX returns in > 60% of instances, or Rank IC < 0.05 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Industrial production (INDPRO) expansion signals improving capacity utilization and manufacturing health, which reduces cost pressures and supports profit margins. This produces positive S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days as the market recognizes sustained supply-side improvement.
regime_hint: post-shock supply recovery with accelerating production
failure_condition: Triggered INDPRO acceleration does not produce positive 20-day returns in > 58% of instances, or Rank IC < 0.03 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST) and retail sales (RSAFS) jointly signal broad-based demand recovery. When both series show month-over-month growth simultaneously, the market reprices growth expectations upward, producing positive S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days. The interaction captures demand breadth that single-series signals miss.
candidate_trigger: HOUST > 1500k units and RSAFS month-over-month growth > 0.3%
regime_hint: synchronized demand recovery across housing and consumption
failure_condition: Joint trigger produces positive 20-day returns in < 55% of instances, or interaction coefficient is not statistically significant (p > 0.10) over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Rising unemployment (UNRATE) signals labor market slack that reduces wage pressures and inflation expectations, allowing the Federal Reserve to maintain accommodative policy. This supports equity valuations through lower discount rates, producing positive S&P 500 returns over 60 trading days in a post-crisis recovery regime.
evidence_refs: fact:UNRATE at 3.7% as of June 2009 but elevated from pre-crisis
regime_hint: post-shock recovery with policy accommodation and labor slack
failure_condition: Triggered instances produce negative 60-day returns in > 45% of cases, or the interaction term is not statistically significant (p > 0.10) over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Declining real interest rates (FEDFUNDS minus CPIAUCSL inflation rate) signal monetary accommodation that reduces the cost of capital for equity investments. This produces positive S&P 500 returns over 5 trading days as the market reprices the equity risk premium downward.
evidence_refs: fact:Real rates compressed in post-crisis environment
regime_hint: monetary accommodation with low real rates
failure_condition: Triggered low real rate periods produce positive 5-day returns in < 52% of instances, or Rank IC < 0.02 over any 18-month rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Real GDP growth (GDPC1) expansion signals sustained economic recovery that supports corporate earnings growth. When quarterly GDP growth accelerates, the market reprices earnings expectations upward over the subsequent 60 trading days, producing positive S&P 500 returns.
evidence_refs: fact:GDPC1 at 22,490.7B as of Q1 2009, post-recession trough
regime_hint: post-recession GDP recovery with positive growth acceleration
failure_condition: Quarters with GDPC1 acceleration do not produce positive 60-day SPX returns in > 62% of instances, or Rank IC < 0.05 over any 2-year rolling window.
